data.history: The Al Hindi family name has complex origins tied to the Indian subcontinent and migration patterns. The most documented case is the Khomeini family lineage, where Ahmad Hindi (1800-1869) was an Indian-born Iranian Twelver Shia Muslim cleric who migrated from Nishapur, Iran to Oudh in northern India, then permanently left India around 1830 to escape colonial rule. He initially went to Najaf, Iraq on pilgrimage, then settled in Khomeyn, Iran in 1834. The family continued to use the nisba 'Hindi' (meaning 'from India') to indicate their stay in the Indian subcontinent. In contemporary times, the surname appears in various contexts including Hanadi Zakaria al-Hindi, the first Saudi female commercial pilot who was trained and supported by Prince Al Waleed bin Talal in the early 2000s. The name 'Hindi' serves as both a surname and a descriptor of geographic origin from the Indian subcontinent across different families and regions.
data.description: Al Hindi is a surname indicating Indian subcontinent origins, used by families who migrated from or had historical connections to India, including notable religious scholars and modern professionals.
data.originStory: The surname Hindi derives from the Arabic nisba (title) meaning 'from Hind or India', indicating families' historical residence or origin in the Indian subcontinent before migration to other regions.
data.modernStatus: The Al Hindi name appears across different professions and regions, including religious scholarship, aviation, and various professional fields, with no unified modern political or business structure.

data.history: The Al Hamli family's documented history is limited to contemporary figures, with no verified historical lineage or tribal origins found in the available research. The most notable member is Iman Ghaleb al-Hamli (born c. 1986), a Yemeni entrepreneur who established solar microgrids in rural northern Yemen during the ongoing Yemeni Civil War that began in 2014. Her work was supported by the United Nations Development Programme as part of efforts to provide clean energy to areas affected by the conflict between the Yemeni government and the Houthi movement. Al-Hamli's microgrid project gained international attention for employing ten local women in conservative Yemen, where women are typically excluded from the workforce. She has used profits from the microgrid to provide small loans to local women for establishing their own businesses, while also advocating for peace, infrastructure rebuilding, agricultural investment, skills development, effective governance, and women's empowerment in Yemen.

data.history: Palm Jumeirah is an artificial archipelago constructed in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, beginning in June 2001. The islands were created using land reclamation by Dutch specialist dredging company Van Oord, designed to resemble a palm tree when viewed from the air. The first residential units were handed over in 2006, with 500 families residing on the island by October 2007. By the end of 2009, 28 hotels were opened on the Crescent. The development has a population of over 25,000 as of 2022 and has become home to a diverse international community of wealthy residents, including Russian oligarchs, Ukrainian businessmen, Iranian steel magnates, Italian fugitives, Pakistani nationals, and members of ruling families from various countries. The ruling Aliyev family of Azerbaijan owns multiple properties on Palm Jumeirah, reflecting the development's status as a haven for international wealth.

data.modernStatus: Palm Jumeirah serves as a luxury residential enclave hosting over 25,000 residents in villas, apartments, and hotel residences, with property values reaching into the millions of dollars and attracting international investors and wealthy families seeking Dubai residency

data.history: The Al Remeithi (Arabic: الرميثي) are one of the constituent branches of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, specifically listed as Al Remeithi/Rumaithat (الرميثات/الرميثي). As part of the Bani Yas confederation, they originated from the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. The confederation, consisting of around 20 tribes, settled in the Liwa oasis around 1700 in present day Abu Dhabi territory. In modern times, the Al Remeithi have maintained their position within the UAE's business and industrial landscape, with notable figures like Saeed Ghumran Al Remeithi serving as Group Chief Executive Officer of EMSTEEL PJSC and Vice Chairman of the Arab Iron and Steel Union, demonstrating the family's continued prominence in the UAE's economic diversification beyond oil.
data.description: The Al Remeithi are a branch of the Bani Yas tribal confederation originating from Najd in Central Arabia. They are part of the larger tribal structure that includes the ruling families of Abu Dhabi and Dubai, with modern members holding significant positions in UAE industry and business.
data.originStory: The Al Remeithi trace their origins to the Najd region of Central Arabia as part of the Bani Yas confederation, which descended from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a. They settled with other Bani Yas clans in the Liwa oasis around 1700 in present day Abu Dhabi territory.
data.modernStatus: The Al Remeithi maintain prominent positions in UAE industry and business, with Saeed Ghumran Al Remeithi serving as Group Chief Executive Officer of EMSTEEL PJSC and Vice Chairman of the Arab Iron and Steel Union, reflecting their role in the UAE's economic diversification strategy.

data.history: The Al Khayat family name appears across multiple Arab regions with documented presence in Lebanon, Kuwait, and the UAE, though the research reveals distinct family lines rather than a unified genealogy. The Lebanese branch, exemplified by the Khayat family of Sidon, traces its origins to Ahmed Al-Zein, who fled to Syria during the Ottoman period under Jamal Pasha's persecution. Ahmed Al-Zein practiced tailoring in Syria and earned the nickname 'Al Khayat' (the tailor), which became the family surname upon his return to Lebanon to continue resistance against Turkish rule. The name subsequently passed through generations, with Tahseen being named after his grandfather who had close ties to Mufti Bahaa Al-Din Al-Zein and married into that religious family. data.history: Al Anzi is a surname derived from the ancient Arabian tribe of Anizah (Anazah), one of the most prominent tribal confederations in the Arabian Peninsula. The Anizah tribe's existence predates Islam, with classical Arab genealogists placing them within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. The tribe was historically divided into nomadic and sedentary branches, with nomadic groups living in northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, while sedentary families inhabited the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd. During the Ottoman era, Anizah became prominent as masters of northwestern Arabian oasis towns, particularly Khaybar and Al-'Ula, collecting tribute from inhabitants while migrating seasonally between Arabia and southern Syria. The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the 20th century severely impacted their traditional Bedouin lifestyle, leading most members to settle within newly formed states and adopt citizenship in Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iraq, Syria, Jordan, and other countries. Today, Al Anzi families are found throughout the GCC region, with some individuals achieving prominence in sports, politics, and other fields, while retaining their tribal appellations as surnames.

data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Anzi families are dispersed across GCC countries, with members active in sports, politics, and various professions. Notable figures include footballers like Mohammed Salem Al-Anzi (Qatar) and Meshal Al-Anzi (Kuwait).

data.history: Based on the research provided, Al Hadban refers to one of the five primary strains of Arabian horses developed by the Bedouin tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. According to the Arabian Horse Association, Hadban (also spelled Hedban) was one of the legendary strains traced through the maternal line, alongside Keheilan, Seglawi, Abeyan, and Hamdani. These strains were developed over centuries by Bedouin tribes who maintained strict oral traditions tracking the ancestry of each horse, with horses of the purest blood known as Asil. The Bedouin believed that crossbreeding with non-Asil horses was forbidden, and pedigree families were traced exclusively through the female line. The strain system was integral to Bedouin culture, representing not just horse breeding but a complex web of bloodline preservation that paralleled their careful tracking of camels, Saluki dogs, and tribal genealogies.
data.description: Al Hadban represents one of the five primary strains of Arabian horses developed by Bedouin tribes, traced through maternal lineage as part of the traditional Asil bloodline system.
data.originStory: According to legend, the five primary strains including Hadban descended from Al Khamsa, the five favorite mares of Muhammad who faithfully returned to him when called, despite their desperate thirst at an oasis.
data.modernStatus: The Hadban strain continues to be recognized in Arabian horse breeding programs worldwide, though modern DNA studies suggest horses with records of descent from specific strains may not actually share common maternal ancestry.

data.history:
The Al Jamri family originates from the village of Bani Jamra in Northern Bahrain, with their lineage documented through several generations of religious scholarship. The family's most prominent figure was Sheikh Abdul Amir al-Jamri (1938-2006), born as Abdul Amir bin Mansoor bin Mohammed bin Abdulrasool bin Mohammed bin Hussain bin Ebrahim bin Makki bin Suleiman bin Makki al-Jamri al-Bahrani. His father Mansoor was a Quranic teacher and textile workshop owner who died when Abdul Amir was ten. The family has a strong tradition of Shia religious scholarship, with Mulla Atiya al-Jamri (1899-1981) serving as a notable khatib (preacher) and poet, who was Sheikh Abdul Amir's cousin and mentor.

Sheikh Abdul Amir al-Jamri became a central figure in Bahraini politics, serving as an elected member of parliament in 1973 until its dissolution in 1975, and later as a judge at the High Religious Court from 1977-1988. He gained prominence as the leader of the 1990s uprising in Bahrain (1994-1999), uniting Islamists, liberals and leftists in demanding democratic reforms. His leadership during this period established him as the spiritual leader of Bahrain's Shia opposition. He spent several years in prison and house arrest due to his political activities, finally being released in 2001 under the reform initiatives of King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa.

The family's influence continued into the modern era through Sheikh Abdul Amir's son, Mansoor al-Jamri (born 1961), who became a prominent journalist and opposition figure. After studying mechanical engineering in the UK and living in exile for 14 years as spokesman for a London-based opposition group, Mansoor returned to Bahrain in 2001 and co-founded Al-Wasat newspaper in 2002. The newspaper became Bahrain's most popular and profitable independent publication until it was permanently closed by authorities in 2017. The Al Jamri family's story reflects the broader struggles of Bahrain's Shia community for political representation and civil rights.
data.description: The Al Jamri family is a prominent Bahraini Shia clerical family from Bani Jamra village, known for generations of religious scholarship and political opposition leadership. They gained particular prominence through Sheikh Abdul Amir al-Jamri, who led the 1990s uprising, and his son Mansoor, founder of Al-Wasat newspaper.
data.originStory: The Al Jamri family originates from the village of Bani Jamra in Northern Bahrain, with documented genealogy tracing back several generations through religious scholars and community leaders.
data.modernStatus: The family remains influential in Bahrain's opposition movement and civil society, though facing government pressure. Al-Wasat newspaper was permanently closed in 2017, and the family continues to advocate for democratic reforms and Shia rights.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Jamri derives from their ancestral village of Bani Jamra in Northern Bahrain, with the family taking the nisba (attribution) al-Jamri al-Bahrani indicating their geographic and cultural origin.

data.history:
The Al Nabhan family encompasses several distinct lineages across the Middle East with no established genealogical connection. The most historically significant branch is the Nabhani dynasty that ruled Oman from 1154 to 1624, emerging after the collapse of Seljuk control. The Bani Nabhan were an Arab tribe from the interior with ascribed Azdi origins who initially served as governors of Sohar under the Buyids and later the Seljuks. After Seljuk decline, they established independent rule under Muhammed al-Fallah in 1154, controlling the lucrative frankincense trade routes from Dhofar through the Yabrin oasis to Bahrain, Baghdad and Damascus. The dynasty made Bahla their capital and built the impressive Bahla Fort (Hisn Tammah), now a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Their rule declined in the 15th-16th centuries as elected Imams gained ascendancy, culminating in their overthrow by the Ya'Aruba dynasty in 1624, though some Nabhanis retained local power in Jabal al-Akhdar until 1956.

Contemporary individuals bearing the Nabhan surname include Palestinian social media personality Khaled Nabhan (1969-2024), who gained international recognition during the Gaza conflict after a viral video showing him mourning his granddaughter Reem, killed in an Israeli airstrike in November 2023. Nabhan himself was killed by Israeli forces in December 2024. Other notable figures include Lebanese journalist Lara Nabhan, Maltese-Palestinian writer Walid Nabhan, and various other professionals across the region, though no genealogical connections between these modern families and the historical Omani dynasty have been established in the research.
data.description: Al Nabhan refers to multiple unrelated families across the Middle East, most notably the medieval Nabhani dynasty that ruled Oman from 1154-1624, and various contemporary individuals including Palestinian social media personality Khaled Nabhan.
data.originStory: The historical Nabhani dynasty claimed Azdi tribal origins and emerged from the Arab tribes of interior Oman, initially serving as governors under various empires before establishing independent rule in 1154.
data.modernStatus: The Nabhan name appears among various unrelated families across Palestine, Lebanon, Malta, and other regions, with no established political or business unity.

data.history: The Al Hakim family is a prominent family of Shiite Islam scholars from Najaf, Iraq who claim descent from the Alids and belong to a Tabatabaei branch whose scholarly involvement has revolved around Iraq for centuries. Their roots trace back to Jabal Amil in southern Lebanon, a region known for its long association with Twelver Shia Islam. The family was named 'al-Hakim' because one of their ancestors, Ali, was a famous physician. The family faced extensive persecution and executions at the hands of Saddam Hussein's government, with seven of Grand Ayatollah Muhsin al-Hakim's sons killed, six of them on orders of Saddam Hussein. Despite this persecution, the family maintained a prominent role in Iraq and the Middle East, with members serving as leading religious authorities, political leaders, and founding major political parties like the Islamic Supreme Council of Iraq.

Ammar al-Hakim leads the Islamic Supreme Council of Iraq, one of the largest Shia political parties in Iraq.

data.history:
The Al Thaqafi family traces its lineage to the Banu Thaqif (also rendered as Thaqaf), an ancient Arab tribe concentrated around the city of Ta'if in western Arabia (the Hejaz region). The tribe gained prominence during the early Islamic conquests, particularly after their conversion to Islam around 630 CE. Following their embrace of Islam, members of the Thaqif gradually attained high military and administrative ranks in the nascent Caliphate and played crucial command and economic roles during the early Muslim conquests, especially in Iraq. The tribe's political influence continued to increase significantly with the advent of the Umayyad Caliphate in 661.

The family gained exceptional prominence through several distinguished military commanders and administrators. Muhammad ibn al-Qasim al-Thaqafi (695-715) belonged to the Abu Aqil family of the Banu Awf, one of the two principal branches of the Thaqif. He became famous as the Arab military commander who led the Muslim conquest of Sindh (modern-day Pakistan) from 708-711, establishing the first Muslim rule in South Asia. Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf al-Thaqafi (c.661-714), from the same Abu Aqil family lineage, served as a powerful governor under the Umayyad Caliphate, ruling Iraq and the eastern provinces from 694 until his death. Another notable figure was al-Mukhtar ibn Abi Ubayd al-Thaqafi (c.622-687), who led a major pro-Alid rebellion against the Umayyads and controlled most of Iraq for eighteen months during the Second Fitna.

The Al Thaqafi family's influence extended across multiple generations through strategic marriages and appointments within the Umayyad administrative structure. The Abu Aqil family gained particular prestige through al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf, who became patron of the Thaqif tribe and appointed several family members to important posts in Iraq and its dependencies. Historical records show extensive intermarriage between prominent Thaqafi families and the Umayyad dynasty, including al-Hajjaj's daughter wedding a son of Caliph al-Walid, further cementing their political position within the early Islamic state.
data.description: Al Thaqafi is a distinguished Arab family descended from the Banu Thaqif tribe of Ta'if in western Arabia, who gained exceptional prominence during the early Islamic period through military conquests and high administrative positions in the Umayyad Caliphate. The family produced several historically significant figures including Muhammad ibn al-Qasim (conqueror of Sindh), al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf (powerful Umayyad governor), and al-Mukhtar ibn Abi Ubayd (pro-Alid revolutionary leader).
data.originStory: The Al Thaqafi family originates from the ancient Banu Thaqif tribe, which was concentrated around the city of Ta'if in western Arabia during the pre-Islamic period. The tribe initially opposed the Islamic prophet Muhammad but embraced Islam around 630 CE. After their conversion, they became influential participants in the early Muslim conquests and administration, establishing themselves as a prominent family within the Islamic state structure.
data.nameEtymology: Al Thaqafi derives from 'al-Thaqafi' (الثقفي), meaning 'of/from Thaqif,' indicating descent from the Banu Thaqif tribe of Ta'if in western Arabia.

data.history: The research provided focuses on specific members of the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai and Al Thani ruling family of Qatar, rather than a comprehensive family called 'Bin Mohammed.' The Al Maktoum family, descendants of the House of Al-Falasi, has ruled Dubai with Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum serving as ruler since 2006, vice president and prime minister of the UAE. His son Hamdan bin Mohammed Al Maktoum has been Crown Prince of Dubai since 2008 and was appointed UAE Minister of Defence in 2024. The Al Thani family has ruled Qatar, with Mohammed bin Thani (c. 1788-1878) being recognized as the first ruler of the whole Qatari Peninsula by the British in 1868. His son Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani is considered the founder of the State of Qatar. The research also includes information about other 'bin Mohammed' individuals from various Gulf families, including members of the Al Sharqi family of Fujairah and historical figures from different tribal backgrounds. However, these represent separate family lines rather than a single cohesive 'Bin Mohammed' family entity.
data.description: The research covers multiple Gulf families with 'bin Mohammed' patronymics rather than a single family entity, including members of Dubai's Al Maktoum dynasty, Qatar's Al Thani ruling family, and other Gulf royal families.
data.modernStatus: The individuals covered include current rulers and senior government officials across multiple Gulf states, particularly in the UAE and Qatar, holding positions such as rulers, crown princes, ministers, and other high-ranking government roles.

data.history: The Bin Yusuf family represents individuals with diverse origins across different regions, rather than a unified Gulf merchant family lineage. The most prominent documented member is Yusof bin Ishak (1910-1970), Singapore's first president, whose family originated from Sumatra with Minangkabau and Malay roots. His father Ishak bin Ahmad was of Minangkabau descent from Penang who moved to Perak, while his mother came from Langkat, North Sumatra. The family relocated between Perak and Singapore as Ishak worked as a civil servant in the British colonial administration. Another notable historical figure bearing similar nomenclature was al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf (c.661-714), an Umayyad governor from the Thaqif tribe of Ta'if in the Hejaz, though this represents a different lineage entirely. In the contemporary period, the name gained notoriety through Osama bin Laden (1957-2011), born into the wealthy Saudi bin Laden family with Yemeni origins and ties to the House of Saud. These represent distinct family lines rather than branches of a single tribal confederation.
data.description: Bin Yusuf represents multiple distinct family lineages across different regions and time periods, including Southeast Asian political figures, historical Arab administrators, and modern Saudi business families, rather than a unified Gulf tribal confederation.
data.modernStatus: The Singapore branch achieved political prominence through Yusof Ishak's presidency, while the Saudi bin Laden family maintains significant construction business interests and historical ties to the royal House of Saud

data.history: The Yaqoub family name derives from the Arabic name Ya'qūb (يعقوب), which is the Arabic version of Jacob and James. The name was in use in pre-Islamic Arabia and became common throughout Arab, Turkish, and Muslim societies. In the GCC region, the name appears among various families including the Al-Qasmi family with Yaqoob Al-Qasmi being an Omani footballer born in 1990, and the Al-Mukhaini family with Yaqoob Juma Al-Mukhaini, also an Omani footballer born in 1982. The name is also found among Kuwaiti families, with Yaqoub Al-Tararwa being a Kuwaiti footballer born in 1994, and Yaqoub Al-Youha, a Kuwaiti sprint runner born in 1993. The prevalence of the name across the Gulf states suggests it has been adopted by families of various tribal and ethnic backgrounds throughout the region's history.

data.modernStatus: The name appears among contemporary families in the Gulf region, with notable figures in sports and other fields, but no unified business or political presence is documented in the research.

data.history: The Al Salmani family name appears in various contexts across the research, though no comprehensive family history is provided. The name 'Salmani' derives from the Arabic root related to Salman, meaning 'safe' or 'secure,' and is often associated with Salman Al Farsi (Salman the Persian), a companion of Prophet Muhammad. In Afghanistan, the Dalak people have adopted 'Salmani' as their preferred self-designation, claiming descent from Salman Al Farsi, though this appears to be a recent development to distance themselves from the perceived offensive term 'dalak.' The name also appears in Persian contexts, as seen with footballer Yasin Salmani and in architectural references to 'Salmani style' in Riyadh. In Libya, the name appears in the geographical reference 'Sbikhat al-Salmani' (al-Salmani Marsh) in Benghazi, suggesting some historical presence in North Africa.

data.history: The Al Sedairy family represents a prominent lineage in modern Saudi Arabia, with documented involvement in scientific research and disability advocacy from the late 20th century onward. The most notable documented member is Sultan bin Turki Al Sedairy, born in 1955 in Riyadh, who has established himself as a leading figure in medical research and biotechnology in Saudi Arabia. The family's contemporary prominence is closely tied to their contributions to medical research, cancer genomics, and disability research, representing the emergence of Saudi scientific leadership on the international stage.

data.history: The Al Hathloul family name derives from the Arabic 'al-Hadhlūl' and appears to be connected to prominent figures in modern Saudi Arabia. The most documented member is Loujain al-Hathloul, born July 31, 1989, a University of British Columbia graduate who became internationally known for her women's rights activism starting in 2014. Her family includes siblings living abroad: sister Alia in Brussels, Belgium; brother Walid in Ontario, Canada; and sisters Lina and Fawzia who have become human rights advocates themselves. The family gained international attention when Loujain was repeatedly arrested, detained, and tortured by Saudi authorities between 2014-2021 for her activism against the driving ban and male guardianship system. There appears to be a separate royal connection through Prince Hathloul bin Abdulaziz Al Saud (1941-2012), the 32nd son of King Abdulaziz, though no direct family relationship to the activist family is established in the available research.
data.description: A Saudi family known primarily through women's rights activist Loujain al-Hathloul and her siblings, with some members living in exile abroad as human rights advocates.
data.modernStatus: The family is known for human rights advocacy, with Loujain al-Hathloul being an internationally recognized women's rights activist and political prisoner. Sister Lina serves as Head of Monitoring and Advocacy at ALQST for Human Rights, while sister Fawzia fled to Scotland where she was granted political asylum.

data.history: The Al Dawoud family name appears across multiple Middle Eastern contexts with distinct lineages. In Egypt, the family produced prominent political figure Diaa al-Din Dawoud (1926-2011), who was born in the rural Nile Delta village of al-Roda in the Damietta region. His family owned about 100 feddans of land and lived in relatively better conditions than most villagers. Dawoud became a lawyer, politician, and founder of the Arab Democratic Nasserist Party, serving as its secretary-general from 1992 to 2010. Another notable Egyptian bearing a variant of the name was Mohamed Salah Al-Din Al-Halim Zidane, known as Saif Al-Adel, born in 1960/1963, who became a high-ranking Al-Qaeda operative after serving as a colonel in Egypt's El-Sa'ka Forces before his expulsion in 1987. In Jordan, Amina Dawoud Mohammed Al-Mufti (1939-2008) was a Circassian-Jordanian who converted to Judaism and worked as a Mossad spy before being captured by Fatah in 1975. The name also appears in Palestinian contexts, with Mohammed Dawoud being identified as a Hamas Shadow Unit field commander killed by Israel in 2021.
data.description: The Al Dawoud name represents multiple distinct Middle Eastern family lineages, including Egyptian political and military figures, Jordanian-Circassian nobility, and Palestinian militants, with no confirmed connection between branches.
data.modernStatus: The family name appears across various sectors including politics, military, intelligence, and militant organizations in Egypt, Jordan, and Palestine, with no unified family structure or business enterprise

data.history: The Al Dhuhli family traces its origins to the Banu Dhuhl, an Arab tribe that was part of the larger Banu Shayban confederation. The Banu Shayban were a branch of the Banu Bakr tribe, with genealogical records tracing their lineage back to Shayban bin Dhuhl bin Tha'laba bin 'Ukaba bin Sa'b bin 'Ali bin Bakr bin Wa'il, ultimately descending from Asad bin Rabi'a bin Nizar bin Ma'dd bin 'Adnan. In pre-Islamic times, the Banu Shayban were a nomadic tribe that wintered in Jadiyya in the Najd and moved to the fertile lowlands around the Euphrates for summer grazing, ranging from Upper Mesopotamia to Lower Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf shores. During the early Islamic period, the tribe was primarily settled in al-Jazira Province (Upper Mesopotamia) and played important roles in Islamic history, with several prominent figures emerging from their ranks. Notable historical figures from the Al Dhuhli lineage include Isa ibn al-Shaykh al-Shaybani (died 882/83), who created a semi-independent Bedouin state in Palestine and southern Syria during the 9th century, and Khalifa ibn Khayyat al-Dhuhli (777-855), a prominent Islamic scholar, historian, and genealogist from Basra who authored the earliest extant Islamic chronicle. Another significant figure was Ahmad ibn Hanbal (780-855), the founder of the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence, whose family originally belonged to the Banu Dhuhl tribe from Basra before settling in Baghdad.
data.description: The Al Dhuhli family descends from the Banu Dhuhl, a subtribe of the Banu Shayban confederation, historically based in Mesopotamia and known for producing prominent Islamic scholars, historians, and political leaders during the early Abbasid period.
data.originStory: The Al Dhuhli trace their ancestry to the Banu Dhuhl, which was part of the Banu Shayban tribal confederation. The Banu Shayban were originally nomadic Arabs who moved seasonally between the Najd and the Euphrates region, later settling primarily in al-Jazira Province (Upper Mesopotamia) during the early Islamic period.
data.nameEtymology: Al Dhuhli derives from Dhuhl, an ancestor in the genealogical chain of the Banu Shayban tribe: Shayban bin Dhuhl bin Tha'laba bin 'Ukaba bin Sa'b bin 'Ali bin Bakr bin Wa'il.

data.history: The Al Qaisi family appears to have origins in Jordan, as evidenced by Mustafa al-Qaisi who was born in Dhiban District in Madaba Governorate in 1937. The family name derives from the Qaisi tribal heritage, which has historical connections to the Arab Qaisi tribes mentioned in historical sources. According to historical records, Arab Qaisi tribes including the Banu Sulaym and Banu Hilal migrated to North Africa during the Fatimid period, with these migrations occurring around the 10th-11th centuries when the Fatimids brought about large-scale Arab tribal movements. However, the specific Al Qaisi family lineage and their settlement patterns in the Gulf region are not documented in the available research. The family has produced notable figures in various Arab countries including Jordan, Palestine, and Iraq, suggesting a dispersed presence across the Levant and Mesopotamia regions.

data.history: The Al Halabi family name is a locational surname (nisba) denoting origin from Aleppo (Halab), Syria, or those who traded with Aleppo residents. The most historically documented member is Suleiman al-Halabi (c. 1777-1800), born in the village of Kukan near Afrin, Ottoman Syria, into a family of Kurdish descent. His father Mohammed Emin was a Syrian merchant dealing in butter and olive oil. In 1797, Suleiman was sent to study Islamic theology at Al-Azhar University in Cairo, where he also worked as a calligrapher. After completing his studies in 1800, he returned to Syria before traveling to Palestine. While in Jerusalem, he met Ottoman intelligence officers who requested him to assassinate French military commander Jean-Baptiste Kléber. On June 14, 1800, he successfully carried out the assassination in Cairo but was captured and executed on June 17, 1800. The family name has since spread across the region, with notable members including Syrian Prime Minister Muhammad Ali al-Halabi (1937-2016) who served from 1978-1980.
data.description: Al Halabi is a locational surname from Aleppo, Syria, with family members of Kurdish descent who have held prominent positions in Syrian politics and other fields across the Middle East.
data.originStory: The Al Halabi surname derives from Aleppo (Halab), Syria. The documented branch through Suleiman al-Halabi traces to a Kurdish family in the village of Kukan near Afrin, Ottoman Syria, with his father Mohammed Emin working as a merchant in butter and olive oil trade.
data.tribalOrigin: Kurdish descent (confirmed for Suleiman al-Halabi's branch)
data.modernStatus: The family has maintained presence in Syrian politics, with Muhammad Ali al-Halabi serving as Prime Minister of Syria from 1978-1980 and later as ambassador to Moscow (1982-1990). Other family members have achieved prominence in various fields across the Middle East.
data.nameEtymology: Halabi is an Arabic locational surname (nisba) denoting origin from Aleppo (Halab), Syria, or association with Aleppo trade. Variants include Halaby, Haleb, Halep, and Halepovich.

data.history: The Al Minhali family appears in historical records as part of the broader Madhhaj tribal confederation, one of the ancient Yemeni tribal groups that stabilized after the advent of Islam. According to the research on Yemeni tribes, Al-Minhali is mentioned specifically as being located in the north of Hadramaut and is identified as part of the Saad clan, which is one of the three main divisions of Madhhaj (alongside Ans and Murad). The Madhhaj confederation was historically described in ancient Musnad script texts as 'the Bedouins of Sheba' and was part of the Kingdom of Kindah, with the oldest mentions dating back to the second century BC. The family's presence in the northern Hadramaut region suggests they were part of the historical migration and settlement patterns of Madhhaj tribes throughout Yemen and the broader Arabian Peninsula, as members of these tribes are noted to have spread to various countries while maintaining their tribal connections.

data.history: The Dhawahir (Arabic: ظَوَاهِر) is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates whose main centre was the Buraimi Oasis and the town of Al Ain. The tribe traditionally consisted of three subsections: the Daramikah, who populated Hili and Qattara; the Jawabir in Al Ain; and the Bani Saad who lived in Jimi. Numbering 4,500 at the turn of the 20th century, they owned much of the cultivated land and property in the Al Ain oasis, except for Buraimi village itself. The tribe has maintained a strong alliance with the ruling family of Abu Dhabi, the Al Nahyan, and the Bani Yas confederation for centuries. Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founding president of the UAE, had a son named Sheikh Nasser whose mother was Sheikha Amna bint Salah Al Badi from the Bedouin branch of the Dhawahir tribe. The early history suggests they originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah, now in Oman, before settling in the Buraimi region. The tribe faced significant challenges in the 1870s when Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan ('Zayed the Great') began buying date groves and water rights predominantly from the Dhawahir, leading to a rebellion in 1877 that was ultimately unsuccessful.
data.description: The Dhawahir is a UAE tribe centered in the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain, traditionally allied with the Al Nahyan ruling family. They have close relations with the UAE's founding presidential family through marriage connections.
data.originStory: The early history of the Dhawahir suggests they originally populated the area of Al Dhahirah, today in Oman, before becoming a later wave of settlers in Buraimi.
data.modernStatus: The Al Dhaheri family maintains close relations with the leading royal family of the United Arab Emirates through various connections, including Sheikh Nasser bin Zayed Al Nahyan whose mother was from the Dhawahir tribe.

data.history: The Al Khashram family appears in historical records as part of the Banu Muhanna dynasty that ruled the Sharifate of Medina from the 10th to 16th centuries. The most prominent member was Khashram ibn Dawghan ibn Ja'far ibn Hiba, who served as Emir of Medina from 1426-1427 during the Mamluk period. His rise to power came through promising Sultan Barsbay 5,000 gold dinars to secure the position, deposing the previous emir Ajlan ibn Nu'ayr. However, Khashram's rule was short-lived, lasting only one year before he was deposed for failing to forward the promised tribute to Cairo. His son Dughaym ibn Khashram later served as Emir of Medina on multiple occasions between 1465-1478, demonstrating the family's continued influence within the Banu Muhanna dynasty despite the earlier setback.
data.description: A branch of the Banu Muhanna dynasty that ruled the Sharifate of Medina, with Khashram ibn Dawghan serving as Emir from 1426-1427 and his son Dughaym holding the position intermittently from 1465-1478.
data.originStory: The Al Khashram were part of the Banu Muhanna dynasty, descendants of the Husaynid Alids who traced their lineage to Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib and his son Husayn. The dynasty was founded by Ubayd Allah ibn Tahir who seized power in Medina around 940 CE.

data.history: The Al Mousawi family is part of a larger Islamic lineage claiming descent from Musa al-Kazim, the seventh of the Twelve Imams in Shi'a Islam. The name 'al-Musawi' (Arabic: الموسَوي) is an Islamic title indicating descent from this revered figure, making it one of the most widespread and influential Muslim families globally. Members are referred to with the honorific title 'Sayyid' as direct descendants of the Prophet Muhammad through Imam Ali and Fatimah. In the Arabian Gulf region, particularly Kuwait and Bahrain, Al Mousawi families have played significant roles in education, medicine, journalism, and religious scholarship. Notable Gulf members include Kuwaiti educator Muhammad Hassan Al-Musawi (1912-1995), who revolutionized Kuwait's educational system serving as principal of Jafari School from 1942-1973, and Bahraini physician-politician Faisal Al-Mousawi (born 1944) who served as Minister of Health from 1995-2002. The family also includes prominent media figures like Lebanese journalist Ibrahim Mousawi (born 1965) who became Hezbollah's media relations officer, and tragic figures like Bahraini photographer Sayed Ahmed Al Mousawi, an internationally awarded journalist sentenced to ten years in prison in 2014 for covering anti-government demonstrations.
data.description: Al Mousawi is a widespread Shi'a Muslim family claiming descent from Musa al-Kazim, the seventh Imam, with significant presence in Gulf countries including prominent educators, physicians, and religious scholars.
data.originStory: According to Islamic tradition, all Al Mousawi family members trace their lineage through the Twelve Imams back to Imam Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah, daughter of Prophet Muhammad. The family name derives from Musa al-Kazim, son of Imam Ja'far al-Sadiq, establishing their claim as Sayyids or direct descendants of the Prophet.
data.modernStatus: Al Mousawi family members hold prominent positions across Gulf countries in education, medicine, politics, and religious leadership. In Kuwait, they have contributed significantly to educational reform and religious scholarship. In Bahrain, family members serve in medical and political roles, though some have faced persecution for journalism and political activities.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Musawi derives from Musa al-Kazim, the seventh of the Twelve Imams in Shi'a Islam. The name appears in various transliterations including Mosawi, Moussawi, Moosawi, Musawy, Mousawi, Moosvi, and Moussaoui, with different regional variations.

He represents the Al-Qudaimi family through his chess achievements, having won the Arab Chess Championship in 2007 in Taiz, Yemen. His most notable accomplishment came at the 37th Chess Olympiad in Turin, Italy in 2006, where he achieved a perfect score of 7/7 games as first reserve for the Yemen national team, earning an individual gold medal. data.history: The Akhtar family is a prominent Indian film family with roots in Uttar Pradesh's Khairabad region in the Avadh area. The family's literary and intellectual lineage traces back to Fazl-e-Haq Khairabadi, a scholar of Islamic studies and theology who edited the first diwan of Mirza Ghalib and became a notable figure during the Indian Rebellion of 1857. Farhan Akhtar's ancestors belonged to Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, and he comes from a long lineage of Urdu poets, being the grandson of Urdu poet Jan Nisar Akhtar and great-grandson of Urdu poet Muztar Khairabadi. The family established itself in Mumbai's film industry through Javed Akhtar, who became one of India's most celebrated poets, lyricists, and screenwriters, forming the successful writing duo Salim-Javed with Salim Khan. Javed's children, Farhan and Zoya Akhtar, have continued the family tradition in cinema, with Farhan establishing Excel Entertainment production company in 1999 and Zoya founding Tiger Baby Films in 2015. The family is known for their atheist beliefs and secular outlook, having grown up in an environment where they do not follow any particular religion.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Wadai Sultanate (Arabic: سلطنة وداي Saltanat Waday) was an African sultanate that emerged in the seventeenth century under Abd al-Karim, who overthrew the ruling Tunjur people around 1635. The sultanate was located east of Lake Chad in present-day Chad and the Central African Republic, with its capital at Wara (later Abéché). The ruling dynasty, known as the Kolak dynasty, made Islam the state religion and expanded through strategic marriages with local tribes including the Masalit and Dajo. The sultanate reached its apogee during the reign of Muhammad Sabun (1804-c.1815), profiting from trans-Saharan trade routes and expanding southward. Under Muhammad al-Sharif and his successors, Wadai maintained close relations with the Senussi Islamic brotherhood and controlled much of the Chad Basin until French colonial conquest in 1906-1910. The sultanate was reconstituted under French suzerainty in 1935 and continues as a non-sovereign monarchy in modern Chad, with Cherif Abdelhadi Mahdi serving as the current Kolak since 2019.

data.history: The Matar family name appears in multiple contexts across the Middle East, with notable figures in different countries and backgrounds. In Bahrain, Matar Ebrahim Ali Matar emerged as a prominent political figure, born in Al Daih village in 1976 to a large family with diverse political views - his father Ebrahim was married to three women and had 17 children besides Matar, who was the fifth child out of his mother's seven. The family was politically active, with most members belonging to opposition groups Al Wefaq (Shia) or Wa'ad (secular). Matar pursued higher education in Kuwait, earning a Master's degree in Computer Science (Artificial Intelligence) from Kuwait University, and returned to Bahrain in 2002 following political reforms initiated by King Hamad. He joined the Al Wefaq political party, established its Youth Center, and became head of it, later winning a parliamentary seat in 2010 with 85.72% of the vote, representing Bahrain's largest constituency in the Northern Governorate. His political career was marked by his participation in the 2011 Bahraini uprising, subsequent arrest and alleged torture, and eventual acquittal in 2012. Separately, the name Matar appears in other contexts, including Syrian activist Ghiath Matar who became known for giving flowers to soldiers in Darayya and was killed in 2011, and Hadi Matar, the Lebanese-American who attacked author Salman Rushdie in 2022.

His family background shows diverse political ideologies including leftists, Islamists, Communists, conservatives and liberals, indicating a complex family heritage within Bahraini society.
data.modernStatus: The Bahraini Matar family maintains political prominence through Matar Ebrahim Ali Matar, who continues pro-democracy activism after his 2012 acquittal. He has authored articles for the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, testified before US Congress human rights commissions, and received the 2012 Leaders of Democracy Award from the Project on Middle East Democracy.

data.history: The Sharif family is a Pakistani political family of Punjabi-Kashmiri background that has played a prominent role in Pakistani politics since the late 1980s. The family originates from the Kashmir Valley, with their ancestors migrating from Anantnag in Jammu and Kashmir for business purposes. They eventually settled in the village of Jati Umra in Amritsar district, Punjab, at the beginning of the twentieth century. Muhammad Sharif's mother's family came from Pulwama in Kashmir. Following the partition of India and Pakistan's independence in 1947, the family migrated from Amritsar to Lahore, where they have remained settled as the capital of Punjab. The family's patriarch Muhammad Sharif founded the Ittefaq Group in 1939 after selling his farming land to establish a small steel foundry, later expanding to create the Sharif Group. The family rose to political prominence through Nawaz Sharif, who became a three-time Prime Minister of Pakistan, and his brother Shehbaz Sharif, who currently serves as Prime Minister from 2024. The family has held leadership of the Pakistan Muslim League-N for most of the party's existence and previously led the Islamic Democratic Alliance.
data.description: A prominent Pakistani political family of Punjabi-Kashmiri origin that has dominated Pakistani politics since the late 1980s, with two brothers serving as Prime Ministers and controlling major business conglomerates including the Ittefaq and Sharif Groups.
data.originStory: The Sharif family traces its origins to Anantnag in the Kashmir Valley. Their ancestors migrated from Kashmir for business purposes and settled in the village of Jati Umra in Amritsar district, Punjab, at the beginning of the twentieth century. The family's maternal lineage comes from Pulwama in Kashmir. Following Pakistan's independence in 1947, Muhammad Sharif and his family migrated from Amritsar to Lahore during the partition.
data.modernStatus: The Sharif family currently dominates Pakistani politics with Shehbaz Sharif serving as Prime Minister since March 2024. They control major business conglomerates including the Ittefaq Group and Sharif Group, and lead the Pakistan Muslim League-N party. Family members hold key political positions including Maryam Nawaz as Chief Minister of Punjab and Hamza Shahbaz as a prominent political figure.

data.history:
The Al Hajjaj family traces its origins to the historic Umayyad period figure Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf ibn al-Hakam ibn Abi Aqil al-Thaqafi (c. 661–714), a powerful governor who served the Umayyad Caliphate. Born in Ta'if in the Hejaz around 661, he belonged to the Abu Aqil family, part of the Banu Awf branch of the Thaqif tribe. The family's ancestry was not particularly distinguished, described as poor with members working as stone carriers and builders before rising to prominence through Al-Hajjaj's service to the caliphate.

Al-Hajjaj began his career as a schoolmaster in Ta'if before entering military service under the Umayyads. He rose through the ranks to become one of the most powerful figures in the Islamic world, serving as governor of the Hejaz (692-694) and later as the practical viceroy of Iraq and the eastern provinces of the caliphate from 694 until his death in 714. His administration was marked by significant reforms including the minting of Islamic coinage, changing administrative languages from Persian to Arabic, and overseeing major military campaigns that expanded Umayyad territory.

The family maintained political influence through strategic marriages within the Umayyad dynasty, with Al-Hajjaj's daughter wedding a son of Caliph al-Walid, and his brother's daughter marrying the future caliph Yazid II. After Al-Hajjaj's death in 714 in Wasit, his descendants continued to live in various parts of the Islamic world, with some recorded as living in Damascus and Basra as late as the 9th century. The family name has been preserved through various branches, though the research does not provide specific information about modern Al Hajjaj families in the Gulf region.
data.description: The Al Hajjaj family descends from the influential Umayyad governor Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf al-Thaqafi (c. 661-714), who served as viceroy of Iraq and the eastern Islamic provinces. The family belonged to the Thaqif tribe and rose from humble origins to become one of the most powerful administrative dynasties of the early Islamic period.
data.originStory: The family originates from Ta'if in the Hejaz (western Arabia), where Al-Hajjaj ibn Yusuf was born around 661. His ancestors belonged to the Abu Aqil family of the Banu Awf branch of the Thaqif tribe, initially working as stone carriers and builders before achieving prominence through government service.

data.history: The Al Hadrami family traces its origins to the Hadramawt region of southern Arabia, with documented members spanning from medieval Islamic scholars to modern times. The most prominent historical figure was Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥasan al-Murādī al-Ḥaḍramī, an 11th-century Islamic theologian and jurist born in Kairouan, Tunisia around 1030s to an Arab family from Hadramawt. He served as Qadi in Azougui, Mauritania under the Almoravids until his death in 1095. Another significant Al Hadrami figure was the renowned 14th-century historian and sociologist Ibn Khaldun (Abū Zayd 'Abdu r-Rahman bin Muhammad bin Khaldūn Al-Hadrami), born in Tunis in 1332. Ibn Khaldun claimed descent from Hadramawt through the Arabian Peninsula, specifically tracing his lineage to Wa'il ibn Hujr, a companion of Prophet Muhammad. His family had emigrated from al-Andalus to Tunisia after the fall of Seville in 1248. In more recent times, the family has been represented by figures like Abu Hajer al-Hadhrami (Ghalib Ahmed Baquaiti), a Yemeni jihadist poet born in Mukalla in 1986 who claimed Hadrami ancestry and was killed in a U.S. airstrike in 2015.
data.description: The Al Hadrami family originates from the Hadramawt region of southern Arabia, with documented members including medieval Islamic scholars, historians, and modern figures across North Africa, al-Andalus, and Yemen spanning nearly a millennium.
data.originStory: The family claims origins in the Hadramawt region of southern Arabia, with some branches tracing lineage to Wa'il ibn Hujr, a companion of Prophet Muhammad. Various family branches migrated from Hadramawt to different regions including North Africa, al-Andalus, and maintained presence in Yemen.
data.modernStatus: The family has produced scholars, theologians, historians, and cultural figures across the Arab world, with some branches involved in various political and religious movements in modern times.

folkLegends.title: Al-Hadrami and the Almoravid Dogs
folkLegends.story: According to Mauritanian oral tradition from the 17th century, al-Hadrami played a decisive role in the Almoravid siege of Azougui by neutralizing the dogs used by the primitive Bafour inhabitants against their enemies. The town was known as Madinat al-Kilab (City of Dogs) because the Bafour hunted antelopes and fought enemies using packs of dogs. Local tradition holds that al-Hadrami's intervention allowed the Almoravids to conquer the region, though he died during the battle.

data.nameEtymology: Al-Hadrami means 'from Hadramawt,' referring to the ancient region in southern Arabia (modern-day Yemen) known for its frankincense trade and as a source of Arab migration throughout history.

migrationPath.description: Ibn Khaldun's branch of Al Hadrami family emigrated from Seville after its fall to the Reconquista

timelineEvents.title: Death of Imam al-Hadrami
timelineEvents.description: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥasan al-Ḥaḍramī died while serving as Qadi in Azougui, Mauritania under the Almoravids

timelineEvents.title: Birth of Ibn Khaldun
timelineEvents.description: Ibn Khaldun Al-Hadrami, the renowned historian and sociologist, was born in Tunis to a family claiming Hadrami origins

timelineEvents.title: Death of Ibn Khaldun
timelineEvents.description: Ibn Khaldun Al-Hadrami died in Cairo while serving as Maliki qadi

timelineEvents.title: Death of Abu Hajer al-Hadhrami
timelineEvents.description: Ghalib Ahmed Baquaiti (Abu Hajer al-Hadhrami) was killed in a U.S. airstrike in Mukalla

notableFigures.name: Abū Bakr Muḥammad ibn al-Ḥasan al-Murādī al-Ḥaḍramī
notableFigures.title: Imam and Qadi
notableFigures.roleDescription: 11th-century Islamic theologian, jurist, and Qadi of Azougui under the Almoravids
notableFigures.biography: Born in Kairouan to a Hadrami Arab family, he studied with scholars including Abu Imran al-Fasi and briefly in Córdoba in 1094. He served as Qadi in Azougui, Mauritania until his death and wrote the surviving work Kitâb al-Ishâra on governance.
achievements: Served as Qadi under the Almoravids
Authored Kitâb al-Ishâra on political guidance
Became a venerated figure in Mauritanian oral tradition

notableFigures.name: Abū Zayd 'Abdu r-Rahman bin Muhammad bin Khaldūn Al-Hadrami
notableFigures.title: Historian and Sociologist
notableFigures.roleDescription: 14th-century Arab historian, philosopher, and sociologist, author of the Muqaddimah
notableFigures.biography: Born in Tunis to a Hadrami family that had emigrated from Seville, he became one of the greatest social scientists of the Middle Ages. He served various rulers across North Africa and al-Andalus before settling in Cairo as a judge and scholar.
achievements: Developed theories of social cohesion and historical cycles
Served as Maliki qadi in Cairo
Met with Tamerlane during the siege of Damascus

notableFigures.name: Ghalib Ahmed Baquaiti
notableFigures.title: Abu Hajer al-Hadhrami
notableFigures.roleDescription: Yemeni jihadist poet and munshid, member of AQAP
notableFigures.biography: Born in Mukalla to a Hadrami family, he became known for jihadist nasheeds and poetry. He traveled to Syria in 2007, was imprisoned and tortured, then escaped and joined AQAP in Yemen.
achievements: Pioneer of jihadist poetry in Yemeni dialect
